title: Community ecology
description: Test your knowledge of community ecology!

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table lists the number of individuals of each species in an insect community.

Species | Number of individuals
:-: | :-:
A | $285$
B | $175$
C | $362$
D | $415$
Total | $1{,}237$

**Which of the following values represents the Simpson’s Diversity Index for the insect community?**

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
For the insect community described above, the Simpson’s Diversity Index equation can be expanded as follows:

 $\text{Diversity Index} = 1-[(\dfrac{n_A}{N})^2+(\dfrac{n_B}{N})^2+(\dfrac{n_C}{N})^2+(\dfrac{n_D}{N})^2]$

Where $n_A$, $n_B$, $n_C$, and $n_D$ represent the number of individuals of Species A, B, C, and D, respectively, and $N$ is the total number of organisms in the community.

We can use the values provided in the table to calculate the Simpson’s Diversity Index:

$\begin{align}\text{Diversity Index} &= 1-[(\dfrac{285}{1{,}237})^2+(\dfrac{175}{1{,}237})^2+(\dfrac{362}{1{,}237})^2+(\dfrac{415}{1{,}237})^2]\\\\&= 1-0.27\\\\&= 0.73\end{align}$
The Simpson's Diversity Index for the insect community is $0.73$.

options.explanation:
$\text{Simpson's Diversity Index}:$

$\text{Diversity Index} = 1-\sum(\dfrac{n}{N})^2$

$\text{where:}$

$\text{n} = \text{the total number of organisms of a particular species}$
$\text{N} = \text{the total number of organisms of all species}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table lists the number of individuals of each species in a freshwater fish community.

Species | Number of individuals
:-: | :-:
A | $675$
B | $156$
C | $223$
D | $278$
Total | $1{,}332$

**Which of the following values represents the Simpson’s Diversity Index for the freshwater fish community?**

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
For the freshwater fish community described above, the Simpson’s Diversity Index equation can be expanded as follows:

$\text{Diversity Index} = 1-[(\dfrac{n_A}{N})^2+(\dfrac{n_B}{N})^2+(\dfrac{n_C}{N})^2+(\dfrac{n_D}{N})^2]$

Where $n_A$, $n_B$, $n_C$, and $n_D$ represent the number of individuals of Species A, B, C, and D, respectively, and $N$ is the total number of organisms in the community.

We can use the values provided in the table to calculate the Simpson’s Diversity Index:

$\begin{align}\text{Diversity Index} &= 1-[(\dfrac{675}{1{,}332})^2+(\dfrac{156}{1{,}332})^2+(\dfrac{223}{1{,}332})^2+(\dfrac{278}{1{,}332})^2]\\\\&= 1-0.34\\\\&= 0.66\end{align}$
The Simpson's Diversity Index for the freshwater fish community is $0.66$.

options.explanation:
$\text{Simpson's Diversity Index}:$

$\text{Diversity Index} = 1-\sum(\dfrac{n}{N})^2$

$\text{where:}$

$\text{n} = \text{the total number of organisms of a particular species}$
$\text{N} = \text{the total number of organisms of all species}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table lists the number of individuals of each species in a rainforest tree community.

Species | Number of individuals
:-: | :-:
A | $165$
B | $654$
C | $120$
D | $196$
Total | $1{,}135$

**Which of the following values represents the Simpson’s Diversity Index for the rainforest tree community?**

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
For the rainforest tree community described above, the Simpson’s Diversity Index equation can be expanded as follows:

$\text{Diversity Index} = 1-[(\dfrac{n_A}{N})^2+(\dfrac{n_B}{N})^2+(\dfrac{n_C}{N})^2+(\dfrac{n_D}{N})^2]$

Where $n_A$, $n_B$, $n_C$, and $n_D$ represent the number of individuals of Species A, B, C, and D, respectively, and $N$ is the total number of organisms in the community.

We can use the values provided in the table to calculate the Simpson’s Diversity Index:

$\begin{align}\text{Diversity Index} &= 1-[(\dfrac{165}{1{,}135})^2+(\dfrac{654}{1{,}135})^2+(\dfrac{120}{1{,}135})^2+(\dfrac{196}{1{,}135})^2]\\\\&= 1-0.39\\\\&= 0.61\end{align}$
The Simpson's Diversity Index for the rainforest tree community is $0.61$.

options.explanation:
$\text{Simpson's Diversity Index}:$

$\text{Diversity Index} = 1-\sum(\dfrac{n}{N})^2$

$\text{where:}$

$\text{n} = \text{the total number of organisms of a particular species}$
$\text{N} = \text{the total number of organisms of all species}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Populations of wolves and moose coexist in a certain North American wilderness area. In this area, the moose are a primary food source for the wolves.

**Which of the following models best predicts the effect over time if, due to a policy change, humans are given permission to hunt wolves in the wilderness area?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The wolf and moose populations have a predator-prey relationship.
So, if the size of the wolf population decreases due to hunting, then the moose will have fewer predators. As a result, the moose population will likely increase.

So, the model that best predicts how the introduction of wolf hunting will affect the populations over time is:

[[☃ image 1]]

choices.content: ![A line graph of a wolf population and a moose population in an area from the year 2000 to the year 2020. An arrow pointing to the line indicates that wolf hunting is introduced in 2003. From 2003 to 2020, the wolf population decreases from 20 to 4 and the moose population increases from 22 to 100.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/16ba0db49a3cde7a1fd9291ad82cb330c9c179ee)

![A line graph of a wolf population and a moose population in an area from the year 2000 to the year 2020. An arrow pointing to the line indicates that wolf hunting is introduced in 2003. From 2003 to 2020, the wolf population decreases from 20 to 4 and the moose population decreases from 100 to 5.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a5212f3586c27cf69ba1feb6a23f3aa1e109bcbc)

![A line graph of a wolf population and a moose population in an area from the year 2000 to the year 2020. An arrow pointing to the line indicates wolf hunting is introduced in 2003. From 2003 to 2020, the wolf population decreases from 20 to 4 and the moose population remains constant at 60.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/594d8603278ad668fb997e43398f55f1f7c33654)

![A line graph of a wolf population and a moose population in an area from the year 2000 to the year 2020. An arrow pointing to the line indicates that wolf hunting is introduced in 2003. From 2003 to 2020, the wolf population decreases from 20 to 4 and the moose population increases from 70 to 100 in 2010 before rapidly decreasing and flatlining at 10 in 2015.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/453974b6ef5f59dfd2d71c0988238fa985e53c4a)

assessment_items.question_content:
In a certain wilderness area, rabbits are a major food source for coyotes. At a certain point in time, a disease outbreak severely affects the coyote population. The population does recover, but only after many years.

**Which of the following graphs best models the effect of the disease outbreak and subsequent recovery on the coyote and rabbit populations?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The coyote and rabbit populations have a predator-prey relationship.
So, if the size of the coyote population decreases due to disease, then the rabbits will have fewer predators. As a result, the rabbit population will likely increase.
Then, if the coyote population recovers and grows in number, the rabbits will have more predators. As a result, the rabbit population will likely decrease.

So, the graph that best models the effect of the disease outbreak and subsequent recovery on the coyote and rabbit populations is:

[[☃ image 1]]

choices.content: ![A line graph of a coyote population and a rabbit population in an area from the year 2000 to the year 2020. An arrow pointing to the line indicates that a coyote disease outbreak occurs in 2003. From 2003 to 2011, the coyote population decreases from 15 to 5 and then increases to 15 in 2020. From 2002 to 2012 the rabbit population increases from 40 to 80 and then decreases to 40 in 2020.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3923a10fd98de0e2a32d2825b261f0c8131a5c70)

![A line graph of a coyote population and a rabbit population in an area from the year 2000 to the year 2020. An arrow pointing to the line indicates that a coyote disease outbreak occurs in 2003. From 2003 to 2011, the coyote population decreases from 15 to 5 and then increases to 15 in 2020. From 2002 to 2012 the rabbit population decreases from 60 to 20 and then increases to 60 in 2020.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/32c8a0f68561163f3b5534c0cdcbab93907125be)

![A line graph of a coyote population and a rabbit population in an area from the year 2000 to the year 2020. An arrow pointing to the line indicates that a coyote disease outbreak occurs in 2003. From 2003 to 2011, the coyote population decreases from 15 to 5 and then increases to 15 in 2020. The rabbit population remains constant at 60 throughout.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ea2185d6b4ae744b37e74b2c2816d30ad8882b30)

![A line graph of a coyote population and a rabbit population in an area from the year 2000 to the year 2020. An arrow pointing to the line indicates that a coyote disease outbreak occurs in 2003. From 2003 to 2011, the coyote population decreases from 15 to 5 and then increases to 15 in 2020. From 2003 to 2020 the rabbit population decreases from 115 to 15.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/178ab2f7872b7b5836a859d8f9f41fc4210f7c17)

assessment_items.question_content:
Populations of lions and wildebeest coexist in a certain savannah ecosystem. In this ecosystem, the wildebeest are a primary food source for the lions.

**Which of the following models best predicts the effect over time if humans are prevented from hunting lions in the savannah ecosystem?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The lion and wildebeest populations have a predator-prey relationship.
So, if the size of the lion population increases due to a reduction in hunting, then the wildebeest will have more predators. As a result, the wildebeest population will likely decrease.

So, the model that best predicts how the prevention of lion hunting will affect the populations over time is:

[[☃ image 1]]

choices.content: ![A line graph of a lion population and a wildebeest population in an area from the year 2000 to the year 2020. An arrow pointing to the line indicates that lion hunting ends in 2003. From 2003 to 2020, the lion population increases from 5 to 20 and the wildebeest population decreases from 100 to 15.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f4ab7f95c91ccbab58122d23236f2e801f75deb9)

![A line graph of a lion population and a wildebeest population in an area from the year 2000 to the year 2020. An arrow pointing to the line indicates that lion hunting ends in 2003. From 2003 to 2020, the lion population increases from 10 to 25 and the wildebeest population increases from 10 to 115.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8c931fa219560ef6ac6389687960d97b917fdf95)

![A line graph of a lion population and a wildebeest population in an area from the year 2000 to the year 2020. An arrow pointing to the line indicates that lion hunting ends in 2003. From 2003 to 2020, the lion population increases from 5 to 20 and the wildebeest population remains constant at 65.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9fb3ec3413ec21559e58528a41a0c3f00d56bd78)

![A line graph of a lion population and a wildebeest population in an area from the year 2000 to the year 2020. An arrow pointing to the line indicates that lion hunting ends in 2003. From 2003 to 2020, the lion population increases from 5 to 20 and the wildebeest population decreases from 65 to 30 in 2009 before increasing to 110 in 2012 and remaining constant until 2020.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4ddbb92e73eca279c9dc200569e632100b89650a)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following describes a mutualistic relationship?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A puffer fish’s deadly toxin is made by *Vibrio* bacteria that live protected within the intestines of the fish.
Ticks attach to a moose in the fall and feed on its blood throughout the winter, causing the moose to become anemic and weak.
Cattle egrets have increased feeding rates when following cows because they eat the insects that fly up from the grass as the cows graze.
The common spiny mouse and the golden spiny mouse are diurnal species, but in areas where they coexist, the common spiny mouse becomes nocturnal.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes a parasitic relationship?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The brown-headed cowbird lays its eggs in the nests of other birds who then feed and care for the cowbird hatchlings.
Pseudoscorpions can attach themselves to large beetles, dispersing to new habitats as the beetles fly.
A sorghum plant provides sugar to a root-associated fungus that in turn helps the plant absorb water and minerals.
The black-backed jackal and the side-striped jackal can both live in grassland habitats, but in areas where they coexist, the side-striped jackal moves into woodland habitats.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes a commensal relationship?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Epiphytic orchids can grow on sunny branches high in a forest canopy without significantly affecting the trees on which they grow.
A clownfish has a layer of mucus on its skin that allows it to live protected within an anemone’s stinging tentacles, eating the anemone’s parasites.
*Plasmodium falciparum* is a unicellular protist that can grow inside and rupture human red blood cells, causing fever and headaches.
*Paramecium aurelia* and *P. caudatum* establish stable populations when grown in separate cultures, but when grown together, all *P. caudatum* individuals die.

assessment_items.question_content:
Relationships between populations in an ecosystem are often represented using models that show whether a population has a positive or negative effect on the size of another population, and whether this effect is direct or indirect. Common symbols used for these types of interactions are shown below.

[[☃ image 2]]

This type of relationship modeling can be done for kelp forests, which are marine ecosystems found primarily along rocky shorelines of the North Pacific Ocean. In kelp forests, kelp provides a food source for sea urchins, which in turn provide a food source for sea otters.

**Based on the information given above, which of the following models best represents the relationships between kelp, sea urchins, and otters in a kelp forest ecosystem?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
According to the text, sea urchins are a food source for sea otters. This means that sea otters have a *direct, negative* effect on the sea urchin population. This type of relationship can be shown like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

The text also tells us that kelp are a food source for sea urchins, so sea urchins have a *direct, negative* effect on the kelp population. We can add this relationship to our model, like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

If sea otters reduce the sea urchin population, then there are fewer sea urchins in the environment eating the kelp. So, by reducing the sea urchin population, sea otters have an *indirect, positive* effect on the kelp population. We can add this relationship to our model, like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: A legend shows that a plus sign indicates a positive effect on population size, a minus sign indicates a negative effect on population size, an solid arrow from A to B indicates a direct effect of population A on B, and a dashed arrow indicates an indirect effect of population A on B.

choices.content: ![Diagram of 3 levels of ecosystem relationships. A solid arrow with a minus sign points from sea otters to sea urchins. A solid arrow with a minus sign points from sea urchins to kelp. A dashed arrow with a plus sign points from sea otters to kelp.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ba963df8a49c7e53e162d94dafe1a24a.png)

![Diagram of 3 levels of ecosystem relationships.  A solid arrow with a minus sign points from sea otters to sea urchins. A solid arrow with a minus sign points from sea urchins to kelp. A dashed arrow with a minus sign points from kelp to sea otters.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ee438cb23e6a7aa4ae85f924ed0ce717.png)

![Diagram of 3 levels of ecosystem relationships. A solid arrow with a minus sign points from sea otters to sea urchins. A solid arrow with a minus sign points from sea urchins to kelp.
C. A dashed arrow with a minus sign points from sea otters to kelp.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7a31cf2c3b4036dc07372ab453617060.png)

![Diagram of 3 levels of ecosystem relationships. A solid arrow with a plus sign points from sea otters to sea urchins. A solid arrow with a plus sign points from sea urchins to kelp. A dashed arrow with a minus sign points from kelp to sea otters.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7a90a6d36e579c7080e7b46011a60a78.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
Relationships between populations in an ecosystem are often represented using models that show whether a population has a positive or negative effect on the size of another population, and whether this effect is direct or indirect. Common symbols used for these types of interactions are shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

This type of relationship modeling can be done for an arctic tundra ecosystem, such as that found on Alaska’s Aleutian Islands. These islands are home to many seabirds, whose droppings provide a major nutrient source for the islands’ grasses. The seabirds are eaten by arctic foxes, which were introduced to the islands by humans in the 1800’s.

**Based on the information given above, which of the following models best represents the relationships between grasses, seabirds, and arctic foxes on the Aleutian Islands?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
According to the text, seabirds are a food source for arctic foxes. This means that arctic foxes have a *direct, negative* effect on the seabird population. This type of relationship can be shown like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

The text also tells us that seabirds provide a nutrient source for grasses, so the seabirds have a *direct, positive* effect on the grass population. We can add this relationship to our model, like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

If arctic foxes reduce the seabird population, then there are fewer seabirds in the environment providing nutrients for the grasses. So, by reducing the seabird population, arctic foxes have an *indirect, negative* effect on the grass population. We can add this relationship to our model, like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: A legend shows that a plus sign indicates a positive effect on population size, a minus sign indicates a negative effect on population size, an solid arrow from A to B indicates a direct effect of population A on B, and a dashed arrow indicates an indirect effect of population A on B.

choices.content: ![Diagram of 3 levels of ecosystem relationships. A solid arrow with a minus sign points from arctic foxes to seabirds. A solid arrow with a plus sign points from seabirds to grasses. A dashed arrow with a minus sign points from arctic foxes to grasses.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/84c2db620e4e5077a7d3e04aec3d3121.png)

![Diagram of 3 levels of ecosystem relationships. A solid arrow with a minus sign points from arctic foxes to seabirds. A solid arrow with a plus sign points from seabirds to grasses. A dashed arrow with a minus sign points from grasses to arctic foxes.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8c0bccfe82c5c0994ada1d5ee5267065.png)

![Diagram of 3 levels of ecosystem relationships. A solid arrow with a plus sign points from arctic foxes to seabirds. A solid arrow with a minus sign points from seabirds to grasses. A dashed arrow with a plus sign points from arctic foxes to grasses.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0b19f6cf07c23ec1f5ee9368c5355ae6.png)

![Diagram of 3 levels of ecosystem relationships. A solid arrow with a plus sign points from arctic foxes to seabirds. A solid arrow with a minus sign points from seabirds to grasses. A dashed arrow with a plus sign points from grasses to arctic foxes.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ce69fbf2b71582f5071e093af125b59f.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
Relationships between populations in an ecosystem are often represented using models that show whether a population has a positive or negative effect on the size of another population, and whether this effect is direct or indirect. Common symbols used for these types of interactions are shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

This type of relationship modeling can be done for freshwater lake ecosystems, including those in the midwestern United States. In one such ecosystem, zooplankton provides a food source for minnows, which in turn are eaten by largemouth bass.

**Based on the information given above, which of the following models best represents the relationships between zooplankton, minnows, and largemouth bass in a freshwater lake ecosystem?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
According to the text, minnows are a food source for largemouth bass. This means that largemouth bass have a *direct, negative* effect on the minnow population. This type of relationship can be shown like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

The text also tells us that zooplankton is a food source for minnows, so minnows have a *direct, negative* effect on the zooplankton population. We can add this relationship to our model, like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

If largemouth bass reduce the minnow population, then there are fewer minnows in the environment eating the zooplankton. So, by reducing the minnow population, largemouth bass have an *indirect, positive* effect on the zooplankton population. We can add this relationship to our model, like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: A legend shows that a plus sign indicates a positive effect on population size, a minus sign indicates a negative effect on population size, an solid arrow from A to B indicates a direct effect of population A on B, and a dashed arrow indicates an indirect effect of population A on B.

choices.content: ![Diagram of 3 levels of ecosystem relationships. A solid arrow with a minus sign points from bass to minnows. A solid arrow with a minus sign points from minnows to zooplankton. A dashed arrow with a plus sign points from bass to zooplankton.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/70bdae5c174e80843928d5705fce95cd.png)

![Diagram of 3 levels of ecosystem relationships. A solid arrow with a minus sign points from bass to minnows. A solid arrow with a minus sign points from minnows to zooplankton. A dashed arrow with a minus sign points from zooplankton to bass.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fec952f30431af1d9f032649bde933bf.png)

![Diagram of 3 levels of ecosystem relationships. A solid arrow with a minus sign points from bass to minnows. A solid arrow with a plus sign points from minnows to zooplankton. A dashed arrow with a plus sign points from bass to zooplankton.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4d27856beb8fcc1b49abfd5a7df49f0c.png)

![Diagram of 3 levels of ecosystem relationships. A solid arrow with a minus sign points from bass to minnows. A solid arrow with a plus sign points from minnows to zooplankton. A dashed arrow with a minus sign points from zooplankton to bass.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/eb7084f7c85d4cd467593e8abfd7d186.png)
